source_metadata.title_raw: Jose L. Angel, P.E. - A-19-096 - July 22, 2019 - Escondido

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

Jose L. Angel, P.E.
PO Box 461143
Escondido, CA 92046

July 22, 2019

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-096

Dear Mr. Angel:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. As a former employee of a California Regional Water Quality Board ("Water Board"), does the Act's one-year ban prohibit you from providing policy and technical advice to your former employer, One World Beef Packers ("OWB"), on water quality or wastewater matters?

2. Does the Act's permanent ban prohibit you from providing regulatory and technical advice to OWB regarding Water Board Permit R7-2018-0025?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. If you solely advise OWB and do not appear or communicate with the Water Board you will not violate the one-year ban.

2. If you assist OWB in any proceeding regarding Permit R7-2018-0025 before the State the permanent ban may apply. However, to the extent you are providing technical assistance in complying with the existing terms of the permit and there is no future proceeding before the State involving the permit, the permanent ban does not apply.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-096
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You were an executive officer with the California Regional Water Quality Control Board,
Colorado River Basin Region ("Water Board") – a position designated in your former agency's
conflict of interest code.

While at the Water Board, you held various positions before becoming an executive officer,
including senior water resources control engineer specialist, senior water resources control
engineer, supervising water resources control engineer, principal water resources control engineer,
and assistant executive officer.

One of the functions of the Water Board involves issuing permits to applicants. The Water
Board permitting process is quasi-adjudicative in that when the agency adopts a permit, it assigns
rights and responsibility to act to the permittee(s). Typically, a responsible party applies for a permit
or revised permit. The application is handled at the staff level. In consultation with upper
management and assigned counsel, staff drafts a permit based on the application it received. As the
executive officer, your practice was to review every draft permit before it was circulated for public
review and comment to ensure it was technically sound, and it properly applied water quality laws
and regulations to protect water quality. Once the draft permit is approved by an executive officer
and legal counsel, it is circulated for public review and comments for 30 days to ensure affected
parties have an opportunity to comment on the draft.

Staff reviews comments and responds to them appropriately. Sometimes the comments
received trigger changes on the draft permit. Once comments are addressed, the draft permit is
publicly noticed and scheduled for consideration of adoption at a public meeting. During the public
hearing, the Water Board considers the comments received before the hearing and the staff response
to the comments. It also considers the staff's recommendation on the draft permit. The Water Board
may also receive additional comments during the hearing. If the comments received during the
hearing trigger additional and substantive changes to the draft permit, the draft is set aside for
another hearing, after a new draft is circulated for public review and comment. Further, the Water
Board may, on its own motion, make changes to the draft permit even if it is on the consent
calendar. Following a motion and second to consider adoption, the Water Board members vote on
the motion. A simple majority in favor of the motion is needed to approve adoption of a draft
permit.

As executive officer, you worked on the draft of Permit R7-2018-0025 to which One World
Beef Packers ("OWBP") was the applicant. Specifically, you ensured that the draft properly applied
water quality policy and regulations to protect water quality. In this regard, you worked closely with
technical staff and conferred with counsel to ensure the draft was technically and legally sound.
You transitioned the draft permit on to the incoming executive officer when she started working for
the Water Board on August 6, 2018.

You ceased working at the Water Board on August 8, 2018. Permit R7-2018-0025 was
adjudicated by the Water Board based on the executive officer's recommendation on September 20,
2018. After exhausting accrued state leave from August 8 to January 11, you officially retired on
January 11, 2019.

File No. A-19-096
Page No. 3

Several weeks following your retirement from state service, you began working for OWBP
as a civil engineer, dealing with environmental and sustainability issues. The Water Board regulates
OWBP, although you are not aware of any proceedings involving OWBP before the Water Board.
You have not represented OWBP on water quality and wastewater matters before the Water Board
since you began employment with OWBP.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections 87400-
87406 and Regulation 18746.1.)

The One-Year Ban:

Section 87406(d)(1) states:

A designated employee of a state administrative agency, any officer, employee, or
consultant of a state administrative agency who holds a position that entails the
making, or participation in the making, of decisions that may foreseeably have a
material effect on any financial interest, and a member of a state administrative
agency, for a period of one year after leaving office or employment, shall not, for
compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other
person, by making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or
written communication, before any state administrative agency, or officer or
employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented during the 12
months before leaving office or employment, if the appearance or communication
is made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property. . .

Simply stated, Section 87406 imposes a one-year ban on state employees who make, or
participated in making, decisions that could have any foreseeable and material financial effect. The
ban applies for twelve months from the date the employee permanently leaves office or
employment. This ban prevents the employee from representing any other person, for pay, before
their former agency for the purpose of influencing the agency's decisions on general rules, or on
specific proceedings such as a permit, grant, or contract. (Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1.)
Unlike the permanent ban, this ban is not confined to particular proceedings.

The determining factor in applying the one-year ban is whether the employee holds a
position that "entails making or participating in making decisions that may foreseeably have a
material effect on any financial interest." (Regulation 18746.1(a)(4).) You indicated that your
position was designated in your former agency's conflict of interest code. As such, the one-year ban
applies to you.

An appearance or communication includes all of the following:

File No. A-19-096
Page No. 4

• Conversing by telephone or in person
• Corresponding in writing or by email
• Attending a meeting
• Delivering or sending any communication (Regulation 18746.2.)

Although potentially prohibited under the permanent ban, assisting or advising clients or
employers who might appear before or communicate with the official's former agency is not a
prohibited appearance or communication under the one-year ban so long as the former official is not
identified in connection with the appearance or communication. You stated that you solely advise
OWBP and do not appear or communicate with the Water Board. Accordingly, the one-year ban
does not prohibit you from advising your employer as long as you do not appear or communicate
with your former agency.

The Permanent Ban:

The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from switching sides and
participating, for compensation, in a certain proceeding involving the State of California and other
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401, 87402.) The permanent
ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of absence from any office or
employment. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(1).)

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any
oral or written communication – or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or
communication – made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. These types of
proceedings include: any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter
involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency. (Section 87400(c).)
Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that affect the rights or claims of specific parties. It
does not apply to proceedings that involve the making of rules or policies of general applicability.

An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential
information ...." (Section 87400(d).) The permanent ban applies throughout the duration of a
proceeding in which the official participated.

Under the facts presented, you do not intend to participate, for compensation, in a judicial or
quasi-judicial proceeding involving Permit R7-2018-0025 before a state administrative agency.
Your involvement with the permit is limited to providing regulatory and technical advice to OWBP.
You are not aware of any proceedings involving OWBP before the Water Board. So long as Permit
R7-2018-0025 is not involved in a State proceeding, the permanent ban does not prohibit you from
providing technical assistance to OWBP regarding the existing terms of the permit. However, we

File No. A-19-096
Page No. 5

caution that if the permit is subject to any proceeding before the state, the permanent ban may apply
prohibiting you from assisting OWBP with the proceeding.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Ryan O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

87406 and Regulation 18746.1.) 
· 
The One-Year Ban:

making, or participation in the making, of decisions that may foreseeably have a

•
